title: Mullvad vs ProtonVPN: Which Is Actually More Private?
description: A detailed comparison of Mullvad and ProtonVPN on privacy: audit records, no-log policies, jurisdiction, payment anonymity, and RAM-only servers. Real audit dates, real findings.
content:
## The Privacy Question That Actually Matters

Most VPN comparisons focus on speed and Netflix unblocking. This one doesn't. If you care about whether your VPN provider can betray you to a government, a court, or a data broker, the comparison between Mullvad and ProtonVPN is worth doing properly.

Both are genuine privacy-first VPNs. Neither is a scam. But they make different choices about how they deliver that privacy, and those choices matter depending on your threat model.

## No-Log Policies and Audit Records

### ProtonVPN

ProtonVPN has completed four consecutive annual no-logs audits, all conducted by Polish security firm Securitum: 2022, 2023, 2024, and August 2025. The 2025 report states directly: *"The technical evidence reviewed showed no instances of user activity logging, connection metadata storage, or network traffic inspection that would contradict the No-Logs policy."*

Beyond the Securitum reports, ProtonVPN completed a SOC 2 Type II audit in July 2025. SOC 2 Type II is different from a point-in-time penetration test: auditors observe operational controls over a period of months, not just check a configuration snapshot. All reports are publicly downloadable from Proton's website without an account.

### Mullvad

Mullvad has a longer audit history spread across multiple firms. Cure53 audited them in 2018, 2020, 2022, and June 2024. Swedish firm Assured AB (previously Assured Security Consultants) completed a penetration test in 2025 and again in early 2026. The 2025 Assured AB assessment found no critical, high, or medium severity issues.

Mullvad also had a real-world test in 2023 that no audit can replicate: Swedish police raided their offices and seized hardware. Because Mullvad runs RAM-only servers (called Diskless infrastructure), there was no persistent storage to hand over. Police left with servers that held no user data. This is not marketing. It happened.

## Jurisdiction

**Mullvad** is based in Sweden. Sweden is a member of the 14 Eyes intelligence alliance, which sounds alarming until you understand what it actually means in practice: Sweden shares signals intelligence with the NSA, GCHQ, and others. However, Swedish law still requires a court order for domestic legal process, and as the 2023 raid demonstrated, that process yields nothing when there is nothing stored.

**ProtonVPN** is based in Switzerland. Switzerland is not part of the EU or the 14 Eyes. Swiss privacy law is genuinely strong. That said, Switzerland is not a privacy utopia: Swiss authorities cooperate with international law enforcement under mutual legal assistance treaties. In 2021, Proton Mail (the parent company) was legally required to hand over the IP address of a French activist to Swiss authorities, who passed it to France. Proton has since made Tor access a default option and improved their default no-IP-log position, but the incident showed that Swiss jurisdiction has limits too.

Neither jurisdiction is perfect. Mullvad's advantage is not legal geography so much as their operational architecture: if nothing is stored, a court order cannot compel what does not exist.

## Payment Anonymity

This is where Mullvad takes a clear lead for users who want maximum anonymity.

Mullvad requires no email address, no name, and no account creation beyond a randomly generated account number. You can pay with cash by mailing euro notes to their Sweden address along with your account number. You can also pay with Bitcoin, Monero, or other cryptocurrencies. There is no subscription billing that links a card to your identity.

ProtonVPN does accept Bitcoin and some other crypto options, but signup still involves an email address (which can be a Proton Mail address, adding a layer of separation). Credit card and PayPal are the default payment paths. For most users, this is fine. For users trying to prevent any link between their real identity and their VPN account, Mullvad's cash-in-an-envelope option is still the most anonymous method any mainstream VPN offers.

## RAM-Only Servers

Both services run diskless, RAM-only infrastructure. RAM wipes on power-off, meaning seized or confiscated servers cannot be forensically examined for user data.

Mullvad deployed RAM-only servers starting in 2022. The 2023 police raid was the first real-world confirmation that this protection works as advertised.

ProtonVPN rolled out their own RAM-only server infrastructure more gradually, with full deployment confirmed by 2024. Their Secure Core architecture adds an additional hop: traffic routes through servers in privacy-friendly countries (Iceland, Switzerland, Sweden) before exiting, making traffic correlation attacks significantly harder.

## Affiliate Programs and Honest Disclosure

Mullvad has no affiliate program. They have explicitly refused to build one, arguing that referral incentives corrupt privacy recommendations. This means you will find fewer independent reviews recommending Mullvad, not because it is worse, but because recommenders are not paid.

ProtonVPN does have an affiliate program. This article participates in it. That does not change the factual comparison above, but you should know it.

## Which One Should You Choose?

If you want maximum anonymity and are willing to accept a smaller app ecosystem and no referral-driven review coverage, **Mullvad** is the stronger choice. The account number system, cash payments, Monero support, and proven real-world resistance to a police raid make it the benchmark for operational privacy.

If you want a privacy-serious VPN with a polished app, Swiss jurisdiction, Secure Core multi-hop, and a deep audit record that includes SOC 2 Type II, **ProtonVPN** is excellent and the right choice for most users. The Securitum audit series is among the most consistent in the industry.

For everyday privacy, both are trustworthy. For adversarial threat models where your identity must not be linkable to your VPN account under any circumstances, Mullvad's payment architecture wins.

## Bottom Line

ProtonVPN is the better-documented, more feature-complete option and holds its own on privacy. Mullvad is harder to link to your identity and has survived a real government raid intact. Use Mullvad if anonymity is the top priority. Use ProtonVPN if you want a full-featured privacy VPN with proven audit history and excellent apps across all platforms.
